blocks.content: They left the arcade in the late afternoon.

The sun had softened by then, slipping gold across the sidewalks and turning storefront windows into bright rectangles of reflected sky. Cassandra walked beside Ethan with Harold tucked under her arm, and Ethan carried Gerald by one wing because apparently there was no dignified way to hold a duck with financial trauma.
The town sounded different at that hour.
For a while, neither of them said much.
Silence was usually where he lived.
But with Cassandra, he had expected noise. Jokes. Motion. A sentence thrown into the air just to see what it might become.
Instead, she walked quietly beside him, her steps slower than they had been earlier.
He did not know her well enough to know which version of quiet belonged to her.
That bothered him more than it should have.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: The kind that don't ask permission before becoming important.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: You say things like that a lot.
Ethan shifted Gerald to his other hand.
He searched for the right words.
Not because she made him nervous.
But because she made him want to be accurate.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: You say things like you've already thought about them before.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I think about a lot of things.
He looked ahead at the sidewalk.
Cassandra was quiet for a moment.
Then she said, "Maybe. But you notice things people don't expect anyone to care about."
Ethan did not know what to do with them.
They were too kind to joke away.
They reached the corner where she would turn back toward the arcade and he would continue toward The Copper Mug.
Cassandra adjusted Harold under her arm.
Ethan looked at Gerald's crooked bow tie.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That's one way to describe it.
The honest answer came faster than fear this time.
Not bright enough to hide behind.
A car passed between them and the street, briefly filling the space with sound.
When it was gone, the quiet felt closer.
Ethan wanted to ask if he would see her again.
The question rose all the way to his mouth.
Then stopped there, trapped behind every version of himself that had learned not to ask for things too directly.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That was not a nothing face.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Several. Most of them apologize before speaking.
Then, because something about the day had already made him braver than usual, he said, "Will I see you again?"
For a moment, something passed across her expression.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: we're past pretending that would be an accident.
Ethan felt the words land somewhere deep.
She nodded once, like they had signed something sacred.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He has debt. I'll be careful.
She turned and walked toward the arcade, Harold tucked beneath one arm.
This time, Ethan watched until she reached the door.
And he smiled before he could stop himself.
When she disappeared inside, Ethan looked down at Gerald.
The duck stared back, unimpressed by all emotional developments.
The town looked the same as it always had.
But it did not feel the same.
And for the first time in a long time, Ethan did not feel like he was only observing the world.
He felt like he had stepped into it.
Cassandra waited until the arcade door closed behind her before she let the smile fall.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You were about to say everything.
Lena walked around the counter, eyes moving over Cassandra's face with the careful precision of someone checking for cracks.
The bear was soft against her ribs.
Cassandra looked toward the glass door where Ethan had been visible a moment ago, walking away with Gerald in one hand.
This hurt had nothing to do with tightness.
Nothing to do with pills or reminders or promises made over text messages.
This was wanting something she should have had the wisdom to leave alone.
For one second, she considered lying.
She was good at lying lightly.
But this one felt too tender to dress up.
So she opened her eyes and looked down at Harold.
Cassandra shook her head before Lena could speak.
The words came sharper than she meant.
She softened them with a breath.
Lena's eyes shone with the kind of love that made Cassandra want to run and stay at the same time.
That was not what she expected.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: But I'm also thinking he looks at you like he might not survive losing you.
The arcade sounds kept going around them.
Life refusing to quiet down just because something heavy had entered the room.
Cassandra looked toward the door again.
Just the street beyond the glass.
Just the shape of tomorrow waiting somewhere she could not see.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I don't want to hurt him.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I don't want to hurt anyone.
Cassandra pressed Harold against her chest.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: But I also don't want to keep living like every good thing has to be rejected before it can leave.
Maybe because they sounded like something she had been trying not to say for a long time.
Lena wiped quickly beneath one eye, annoyed at herself for needing to.
Cassandra laughed, but it broke in the middle.
Lena pulled her into a hug.
Cassandra stood still for half a second, then hugged her back, Harold caught awkwardly between them.
Over Lena's shoulder, Cassandra looked at the claw machine.
Tomorrow was coming whether she invited it or not.
And for once, instead of fearing that, she wanted to meet it.
